content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I‘m having a very bad mental health day. Please recommend movies that make you feel happy

**Score:** 83
**Body:** Technically any genre - either upbeat/ funny movies or movies that make you appreciate the gift of life. Anything that will motivate me to get out of this slump really. Nothing that focuses on romantic love (i.e., no romcoms) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Best in show 
2. The Birdcage is hands down my favorite comfort movie when I’m full spiral 
3. Drop Dead Gorgeous, although it's a dark comedy it's still one of the funniest movies I've ever seen
4. Pracilla queen of the desert. No romance just three Aussie queens being their best selves across the continent
5. **My Neighbor Totoro (1988)** \- animated fantasy film about childhood wonder

**The Straight Story (1999)** \- uplifting drama where an old man drives 240 miles on a lawnmower to meet his estranged brother
6. What We Do In the Shadows 
7. Napoleon Dynamite is good for days like this imo. The vibe is extremely gentle.
8. Big Fish
9. Happy Gilmore, Home Alone 1 & 2, Horrible Boses, The Hangover, Office Space, Ferris Bueller’s Day Off
10. Austin Powers, any one of them. Silly fun

## 2. a movie where the female protagonist is evil

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Hello! I was wondering, is there a very good movie where the female protagonist is evil? (Other than pearl and gone girl) but like she never gets nice/good at the end like evil all over just evil 

Thank you

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Misery (1990)

Serial Mom (1994)

Monster (2003)
  - Paul is definitely the protagonist of Misery 
2. I Care a Lot (2020)
3. The Last Seduction 
4. To Die For (1995) and Gone Girl (2014).
  - To Die For is such an awesome film!
5. What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? (1962)
6. Misery. Poison Ivy. Hand that rocks the cradle.

7. I care a lot
8. - Audition

- Basic Instinct

- Dangerous Liaisons

- Single White Female

- Fatal Attraction 

- Wild Things

- The Hand That Rocks the Cradle

....

They are all women who present a very sweet facade but it's hiding the evil underneath. 

## 3. Great Movies made by women that are more like Bottoms,The Substance, Fleabag, American Psycho etc..

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Like weird, gutsy and sometimes erotic with a female gaze.

And also having "Not Hot" People representation. 

And even though having feminist undertones it doesn't make that as the whole thing.






### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Portrait of a Lady on Fire
2. Love Lies Bleeding

The Assessment
3. Booksmart.
4. Slums of Beverly Hills 
5. A girl walks home alone at night, portrait of a lady on fire, Winter's bone.

Not erotic or weird but I enjoyed Little miss sunshine, Lady Bird and The edge of seventeen


If you enjoyed Fleabag than Nobody Wants This, Derry Girls.
6. The Bride ticks many of your boxes. I loved it. It was gutsy, creative, and even though I didn’t understand why Maggie Gyllenhal did some things I was thoroughly entertained and didn’t dwell on it too much. I wish more movies like this were made. Think outside of the box, make something unique and fun. 
7. 'The Night Porter' (1974) directed by Liliana Cavani is very dark and weird but it is also a masterpiece.
8. Bound (1996)
9. lisa frankenstein (2024)

promising young woman (2020)

jennifer’s body (2009)
10. The Ugly Stepsister

## 4. Movies about the fear of being perceived

**Score:** 15
**Body:** As a neurodivergent person being around people is extremely scary because it feels like everyone is hyper analysing every single thing I do. It makes me feel super exposed and I end up second guessing my actions every second. 

I was wondering if there are any movies that capture this anxiety enducing feeling? Any recommendation is welcome but a horror movie with this premise would be perfect :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Truman Show

Not a movie but for horror - Black Mirror History of You or A Million Merits
  - Thumps up for History of You
2. Swiss Army Man 
3. I don’t think it’s the same as what you are describing, but I recently related a bit to Take Shelter, Demolition, Bug, the main subject in The Act Of Killing documentary, also to a smaller degree in this aspect The Ghost Writer. also The Number 23 has an atmosphere I can relate to. Dont Say A Word and The Glass House also are movies I relate to in terms of a specific aspect of my experiences, maybe you might feel that way too. The Weather Man, Dinner For Schmucks, Your Friends and Neighbours (tv series) are tragi-comedies that fit as well I think.
4. Adaptation captures anxiety and self-loathing really well
5. While it may focus more on the fear/depression associated with the anxiety, I Saw the TV Glow does an incredible job at showcasing the existential horrors of hiding yourself from the external (social) world.

Just to give a heads up, when i say its an internal existential horror, its very much that taken to its utmost conclusion. But theres genuinely a lot of value in reflecting on what its exploring.
6. Black swan 
7. Black Mirror episode White Bear
8. Pi by Darren Aronofsky 
9. "The Elephant Man" (1980)

John Merrick is born with a severe disfigurement. He earns his living from being perceived as an "other". The themes of voyeurism, paranoia, agoraphobia, and exploitation are significant. David Lynch directs this absolute classic. Anthony Hopkins notable performance.   

"The Cell" (2000)

A personal favourite. Beautiful, horrifying, devastating and thought-provoking.  


"Spider" (2002)

David Cronenberg directs Ralph Fiennes in a very relevant role.  


"The Machinist" (2004)

Christian Bale tackles extreme grief, trauma, and the fear of facing his own truths. More focused on Compulsive denial, but the fear of perception is a theme.  


"Black Swan" (2010)

This movie scares the shit out of me.

## 5. Movies with strong badass female leads

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Movies like The Hunger Games, Tomb Raider. With female leads that are strong and show character development with some romance and emotional content.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Alien & Aliens
  - Hard to beat Ripley in this topic. Plus in Aliens you get Vasquez as a bonus 
    - Hey Vasquez, have you ever been mistaken for a man?
      - Vasquez - No, have you?
      - No, have you? 
2. Kill Bill
3. The Long Kiss Goodnight
4. Mad Max
5. Terminator (Sarah Connor)

Alien (Ripley)

Anna (2019)
6. Ready or not (2019)

Promising young woman (2020)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Marty Supreme doesn't deserve an Oscar nomination, it is a terrible movie.

**Score:** 789
**Body:** Am I supposed to be rooting for this guy? After his entitled whiny ass cons his way through the movie because he sucks as a human being, finally gets to Japan after abandoning the woman who is the mother of his child who has JUST BEEN SHOT, because of him! Loses an exhibition match against a guy who is arguably better than him, throws a tantrum for a rematch, and then instead of making Marty own up to kissing a pig for losing a match the arguably better , more gracious, and more humble Endo agrees to a rematch, which Marty wins, and then proceeds to go home flying high on his win where he balls his eyes out over his child, which he will most certainly abandon again to play ping pong if given a chance.

Did I get all that right? Why should I care at all that Marty won against Endo, the guy who survived a fire bombing, lost his family and hearing, and seems a genuinely nice person. Why am I supposed to believe that Marty has finally learned humility when that most certainly is not how he went out during that last match? Why am I supposed to believe that he really cares about his child after watching him be a completely selfish prick for two hours? 

This is a terrible movie. Other than Marty being just an unlikable prick with zero growth, it's a meandering mess. It wanders from one dumb situation to the next, and the whole dog subplot is just a stand in for having any actual plot. The music choices are just weird for a movie taking place in the 50's, and I don't know if it was just my theater but the music was loud, way louder than the dialogue.

Overall this year's Oscar picks have been a largely huge let down, with just Sinners, Bugonia, One Battle After Another, and Sentimental Value being anything I liked, but Marty Supreme was the worst one. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Am I supposed to be rooting for this guy?"

No.
  - Exactly. No, you’re watching it to see how much worse he can get.
    - I loved how much the movie keeps escalating too. I was exhausted by the end of the film I was cringing so much. 
  - I love when someone watches a film with a terrible person as the protagonist and them complains that they aren’t rooting for the protagonist and call a film bad for that reason.
If it ain’t for you, it ain’t for you.
That’s okay.
2. That Marty Supreme is a real ***jerk***!
  - I wonder if he owns a dog house?
3. Movies don't have to be about good people to be good. 
4. has he learned humility? are you supposed to root for him? not sure why many people seem to need the main character to be good or likable. he does so many crazy nonsense things to get back in it and then he doesn’t even get in it!! marty sucks, he’s a loser, and has to settle for winning that fake tournament. he got what he deserved and now has to raise a baby he doesn’t even want. exactly what he deserved and i love that.
  - My favorite part was after the cops took the necklace he started up with Paltrow again "well you do have more..." I thought wow the balls on this guy!
5. Safdie movies usually feature an unlikeable character in the lead (Uncut gems, Marty). I actually enjoyed the movie. Although I will say that it was slow paced for me.

## 2. What's your take on this flick? I don't know how this one got by me

**Score:** 672

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amazing cosmic horror. 
  - Agree. I love how it doesn’t spoon-feed the audience and leaves the end open to interpretation. I do wish they had kept the original ending though.
  - And one of the best soundtracks in a horror movie \*ever\*. People mostly know the alien theme that became a meme, but this whole soundtrack is such a manifestation of cosmic horror in music.
2. That freakin bear scene. 
  - Na man the bear doesn't scare me. The scene that scares me the most is when that chick is losing her marbles and starts walking and turns into the goddamn shrubbery. 
    - https://preview.redd.it/1gejb87sn5pg1.jpeg?width=244&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=1650e099aef67971c399d93e190dad45ff3c2a97
3. As far as cosmic horror goes, it's up there.
4. This is one of my favorite sci fi films. Hugely underrated. I’ve heard many people even prefer the film to the books, although I’ve never read them. Great acting and visuals. The ending left me thinking. 
5. I randomly caught this in theaters not having any idea what it was about and ended up loving it.
6. One of the best

## 3. Discussion on James McAvoy's incredible range in Split. Why didn't he win an Oscar for this?

**Score:** 568

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Split is not the kind of movie that gets Oscar noms
  - But sinners is?
    - Sinners is a movie about the recently post-slavery American south and racial culture in America in a broader context.

The accademy *LOVE* race issue themed movies over the last 10-ish years.
  - I’d push back against that. Joker got 11 nominations and two wins, and I’d call them comparable in genre.
    - That kind of thing does happen every so often where a film in a genre that usually doesn't usually get attention from the Oscars does in a particular year. And the Joker was also more of a drama than a superhero movie despite the subject.
2. Or in Glass with the scene where he went through all of the personalities one after another in a matter of seconds was awesome. He's a phenomenal actor. 
3. Cause they were not sure which personality gets the oscar?
  - Give it to Patricia and let them fight over it
4. o oscar não é sobre o melhor, é sobre quem faz a melhor publicidade, tipo o Dicaprio só ganhou um oscar mesmo fazendo vários papéis versáteis, vale lembrar de Emilia Pérez, um filme extremamente ruim que ganhou várias indicações e só perdeu devido ao fato da atriz ter falado muita merda na net
5. He split the vote.

## 4. The one Oscar that you think is nailed on. For me, it's Sean Penn for best supporting actor (OBAA).

**Score:** 342

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Why is his shirt so tight?
  - He's not gay!
2. jessie buckley - hamnet
  - Yeah, this one feels like the only real lock in a year where most of the categories are pretty well contested. Screenplays will probably go to Sinners and OBAA.
3. Man I feel bad for other supporting nominations. This guy basically played another lead in his own film with Leo. Iconic performance. Like he blows everyone else in that nomination out of the water
4. He creeped me the F out. Well done. Not easily creeped out. He deserves it. 
  - His walk was diabolical. He should win for that alone imo 
    - You are so so so right. I loved his walk. It was something like he had lifts in his shoes, trying to be macho, and completely insecure. His body language and facial expressions warrant the award regardless…. He was perfect in this movie.

He’s liberal to the extreme, which I’m not criticizing. It seems like he poured everything he hates about politics, the military, and international relations into this character.
5. I'll be honest. I feel like Leo should be nominated for best supporting actor. Because this FEELS like a Sean Penn movie.
6. He's my favorite in the category, my only thought is he splits the vote with Benecio who was also good and then maybe Delroy Lindo gets it.  

## 5. Found this absolute masterpiece in the clearance bin at Walmart

**Score:** 235
**Body:** So good. Pure comedic insanity - I’m building my home movie server and searching out quirky offbeat comedies that flew under the radar. Hit me with your best 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|8K36ms3JrrFuw)
2. ![gif](giphy|1BRXy8cUS1jyw)
3. Spatula city??!!!
4. *Processing img pqvp7tdfz7pg1...*
5. Supplies!
6. We got it all on UHF
7. You so STOOOPID!
8. UHF is a great comedy showing off Weird Al's creativity and sarcasm!  Ghandi 2 would have been a great action movie!
9. Wheel. Of. Fish!!!
10. This is always my answer to “What’s your favorite movie?”.  Can’t take things too seriously sometimes

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A part of the ending to One Battle After Another that didn't sit right with me: the iPhone

**Score:** 184
**Body:** In general I like the movie, but PTA has mentioned how he has been writing this story for 20+ years and that was one aspect which felt very dated to me. 

Part of the epilogue of the film shows Bob at home with Willa, now playing with a new iPhone. She tries to show him how to take a selfie, he can't quite get it right, but at least he's no longer petrified of the modern world. It's all very cute. 

I think my problem with this part is that I feel like PTA is still treating smartphones the way you would treat a cell phone in a thriller from 1999. They're a gadget. The most nefarious thing they can do is be used to figure out your location. But in 2026, it's a post-Snowden, Cambridge Analytica world, and today not only do we know that his paranoia and fears of surveillance are entirely justified, the government and corporations ARE monitoring every single thing you do, tracking you location, reading all your messages etc. But they are also effectively brainwashing people with algorithms and deliberately driving people into bubbles of fear, hatred and delusion. For corporate profit, for political gain, whatever (and tbh someone like Bob is exactly the kind of guy you can imagine going absolutely fucking nuts on facebook or twitter and sharing AI videos of Bill Clinton eating a baby, but that's besides the point)

Compare OBAA to Eddington and how it depicts the state of modern American life & politics, and specifically smartphones. In general I think the film has its finger on the pulse of current events much more than OBAA. In Eddington, all these people living in their own bubbles, doomscrolling all night, recording themselves killing people - the smartphone is an instrument of disconnecting from reality and the world and making the characters into unreachable loons, whereas OBAA uses it to represent a guy letting go of fear and embracing the modern world. The former feels much more appropriate to me. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's really not that complicated. Now that Lockjaw is dead, Bob & Willa aren't in danger. They're a bunch of nobodies. So they don't have to be as obsessive about technology. This also shows that Bob is trying to change as a person.
2. I think it’s a reasonable observation, but for me it doesn’t affect the quality of either film.  Not every movie set in a modern era has to tackle every issue at the same time.  To keep the story focused requires leaving some issues on the cutting floor.

OBAA takes place in its own universe from my perspective, it’s similar to our own but not 1:1.
  - That’s how I felt, like almost-but-not-quite our world. An adjacent reality.
    - Much like the novel it was based on. 
  - The alternate universe thing is how I took it and how the discourse seems to miss? I presumed the film opens in the 2010s and then skips to 2025ish. However I saw a lot of comments from people thinking it started now then skipped to the 2030s and thought it didn't look "futuristic" enough
3. Eddington is a movie about how the powers that be manipulate people through social media.

OBAA is not a movie about social media.

Immigration is a pretty big issue right now. You could change your thesis with one word to flip it:

“Compare OBAA to Eddington and how it depicts the state of modern American life & politics, and specifically Immigration.”

4. Notice how Sensei and his "underground railroad" all use cell phones and STILL are able to be effective activists? And even without cell phones, almost all of the French 75 still got captured/killed. I think PTA's point is less that technology isn't dangerous, and more that we shouldn't be walling ourselves off from the very communities we're trying to help.
5. I don’t think OBAA was supposed to be “connected to reality”. Certainly not the way Eddinton was. It seemed more like a fairy tale. (The skateboarded on the roof, Sean Penn’s entire character etc)

They both felt like slightly askew versions of our world but Eddinton was the only one that seemed to me to be trying to he “grounded” at all 
6. We really don't have enough information to know what the iPhone is for. Bur now he knows he doesn't have to worry about his daughter not being able to take care of herself, it makes sense that he'd rather take on whatever risks he's taking on in order to stay connected to her.

 And I disagree about Bob being the kind of person who would just go nuts on the internet. Bob's on the cautious/paranoid side and you have to assume he's got (lots of) rules for when and where he will use the phone.
7. It’s not a movie about technology.  PTA uses technology as a tool to illustrate the bond between a father and daughter.

OBAA touches on multiple themes and illustrates many issues, but it is in many ways a story about a father and a daughter.

A “father-daughter epic” if you will, about what it means to raise a kid as a solo parent and let them free into the world, and how that process with Willa “healed” and led to growth from Bob.

The phone at the end symbolizes that bond, his growth and her role in helping him get there.

## 2. Two questions about the inner life of David Lynch

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Putting these two together as I believe there is not a little cross over. To avoid making this longer than it already is, I’ve refrained from citing the films specifically and too much biographical detail.

1 - What did he mean by “the art life”? 

He was dedicated and diligent to his craft. For example, in his early days with Jack Fisk, he was painting regularly and for long hours, to the detriment of all else; studies, diet, money, relationships. Later, he was similarly dedicated to his meditation (TM), though found a balance and channeled a use for it.

He respected and even revered ideas, the source they floated in from and the deep spiritual duty of developing them.

These seem to have been his priorities, underpinning what drove “the art life”.

Perhaps there was a certain impulsiveness flowing from all this, particularly that sacred duty to ideas.

He spoke of the art life being incompatible with regular family life, round about his time with his first wife Peggy Reavey was coming to an end, even though she was an artist herself and understood his priorities. 

His daughter, Jennifer, said of his infidelity that he wasn’t driven by malice but romanticism. Falling in love, whether with a woman or an idea, was the greatest thing. Was this the ultimate heart of “the art life”?

2 - What explains the darkness in his work?

All who came into contact with Lynch describe him as a beacon of light and positivity. An extraordinarily kind, considerate and generous man. He avoided and shut out negativity and bad vibes, often making decisions based purely on good feelings. For most cast and crew who worked with him, his sets were their most wonderfully cherished experiences in the film world, setting a benchmark for kindness and pleasantness which no other came close to. Actors gave themselves over to him utterly, trusting not only his vision but also his tenderness in looking after them in extreme vulnerability.

And yet his work is so frequently about violence against women, sexual and psychological abuse and even incest, the evil that men do, portals into the darkest realms of human existence. He had a curious, matter of fact, unblinking fascination with body parts, internal organs and corpses, often deploying and depicting these with shocking frankness. Given the man described above, where did all this come from?

He spoke of his keen awareness, during his childhood and adolescence and before coming to TM, of a deep dark side in nature: things rotting, devouring and dying. He was also similarly curious about and aware of darkness happening behind the closed curtains of polite society. He spoke of crushing anxiety in those early days and also an anger which dwelt within, only alleviated by TM and perhaps his work. So was his work a form of self therapy? That doesn’t quite stack up given the effect of audiences and cast.

Aside from this uneasy awareness, there was one major incident which likely fuelled this darkness: coming across a naked, bleeding, apparently assaulted and likely abused woman coming out of the darkness in the picture perfect suburban town he lived in as a young teenager. This incident both confirmed to him that darkness was indeed happening behind curtains and it also left him with a confused state of helplessness. He tried to console her but was too young and was ill equipped. 

So was the unrelenting darkness in his work merely a meditation on this side of existence, the duality of man, and a means of processing it? It seems unbelievable that a man so completely dedicated to positivity in his life would go to such extreme dark places in his work, putting actors and audiences through such psychological experiences. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 1. I think "The Art Life" means dedicating your whole life to art. He said something like "Smoke cigarettes, drink coffee, and paint, that's it". I think what you can take from that is no distractions, no compromises, and only a couple vices, but otherwise the only thing you do is art.

2. I believe that David Lynch was someone who knew how to channel his emotions, at least that's the impression I get. I do believe that there was a deep darkness within him, and that darkness exists in everyone. Lynch was able to tap into that darkness and exorcise it into his art, freeing himself of it in his day to day life. If you look at Blue Velvet, Jeffrey is clearly based on Lynch and he too has a deep darkness to him. He's a guy that sneaks into a woman's house and watches her get undressed. To me, that darkness is personified in Frank and the film is about him expunging it

All that said, I think there is an element of mythologizing with Lynch. I do think he's one of the greatest artists of our time but he was still human. He cheated on his partner and I don't think there's a lot more to it. He also clearly could lose his temper on sets as seen in the making of documentaries of Inland Empire and The Return
2. I suppose this might be a cop-out, but honestly, if you want an in-depth examination of David's definition of "The Art Life" you should probably just watch the eponymous [biographical documentary film](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1691152). It's a must-watch for Lynch-heads, surely.
3. > His daughter, Jennifer, said of his infidelity that he wasn’t driven by malice but romanticism. Falling in love, whether with a woman or an idea, was the greatest thing. Was this the ultimate heart of “the art life”?

This is the bullshit copout all eccentrics use to ease out of guilt.  I didn't know he was unfaithful.  That's disappointing.

Other than that I don't think it's that complicated.  Artists that live this kind of life put their work above all else in order of importance.  I think it's what they live for and everything else is collateral damage, tbh.  People that are more aware of this and more sensible would go it alone to lessen the hurt, but apparently a lot of people are selfish. 

As per the other stuff, really, you answered your own questions.  The film answers all these questions:  he was working out his feelings through his work.  It's what art is, it's what art does.

I don't think it's unbelievable.  The whole point is to convert all this shit into something positive, something light.  Something that's palatable and is a life force.  And he didn't sugarcoat it and it isn't candyglass, it's unsettling because you're familiar with it.  

He didn't experience all this and perpetuate by putting more of it into the world, he converted it through dealing with it through storytelling and that permeates through other beings throughout history.  It's part of the point of all this.

Edit:  [I'd like to say these thoughts are original but they're not.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ApEh9Sm4BR0&t=8368s)  However, I don't mind passing it along.
  - What I find interesting is that despite his penchant for infidelity, none of his past partners have bad things to say about him. Obviously as an outsider, it's impossible to know just what exactly this means. But it does at least seem that there is some truth to what his daughter said.  
This is not to argue the morality of his actions, but to gain some honest insight into what Lynch was actually like as a person.
    - I'm sure there's some nugget of "for the greater good" mentality but to still jerk people around and play with their emotions is unbelievably shitty.

Like, I love David.  And I've enjoyed his eccentricities, his work, his outlook but god damn.  It's not fuckin' hard to not fuck around.  If people are cool with it, that's one thing, but to enter into a marriage and have kids willy nilly and drag them along haphazardly, I do not understand.  I don't know if some people were under the kook spell and just accepted it.
4. > It seems unbelievable that a man so completely dedicated to positivity in his life would go to such extreme dark places in his work, putting actors and audiences through such psychological experiences.

Agreed, it is unbelievable, and probably a sign that your conception of him is wrong. We have plenty of famous viral clips of him being a crank, going off about fucking phones or fucking time cuts or whatever else. Everything you are saying about him operating purely on good vibes seems to be a complete fabrication, or at least does line up with what I have seen with my own eyes.

He was a good dude, but he was doing his best to manage the competing forces inside of him just like everybody else. The darkness is in his work because obviously it was in him. Movies like Eraserhead and Blue Velvet that show extreme ambivalence about parenthood, selfishness, sadomasochistic arousal tendencies, etc are him directly working with the raw stuff that life is giving him. I see no other alternative, the art is too vividly realized and searching.
5. Haha, no. Art life is about making art, that’s all. You can do that alone on an island with your imaginary friends or in a room full of strangers. You can be intimate with people or have no interest in any bodily activity. I’d even encourage you to try making art yourself. You’ll soon see that it brings your priorities to the surface, and they may be different from his.

About your other question, I think that’s what Carl Jung called the Shadow: a part of the psyche that absorbs material driven out by social restraint. Usually, it’s repressed, but an artist should tap into it to make the work feel real. I think that’s what Lynch was doing.

And in general, do you really want to see what his inner world is about? You can do that, to some extent, but it requires work and patience. You have to listen without judgment, make assumptions, trace patterns, and then reexamine them. For example, your framework relies on the supposition that there is positivity, and that all other things should be driven out as “negative” or as having “bad vibes.” I’m not saying there’s anything wrong with that, but would he see it that way? If not, then how would he see it?

Be careful about causality, too. He had a traumatic incident, but how often does one incident determine the whole work? Think about timing. Which version of Lynch are we talking about? What was happening in his life? What was the overall context?

And just be ready for the possibility that your examination may transform the way you see him. Not in the sense that you dig up some dirt and hate him forever, but in a very human way. Richard Ellmann, for example, started seeing James Joyce differently once he began writing a book about him. Take a look at Zachary Leader’s book about Ellmann. It’s pretty interesting.
6. And I’ll try to explain the dark part. I can’t speak for everyone, but when you write or film horror, it’s often about being sensitive to certain aspects of life. You’re not indulging in them, you’re bringing them onto the page, and you’ll often be the first person to feel uneasy, anxious, or scared by them. Kafka was a lawyer and knew how the cogwheels of the institutional machine turned, and that’s why he could write The Trial, to show how a person could be reduced to a file. It’s life experience with the imagination running wild.

## 3. Every time I watch a Godard movie I feel like I'm sick of what he has to say, then I watch another a month later - what's the next best step in his filmography after the 6 I've already watched?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I've seen Contempt, Masculine Feminine, Pierrot le Fou, Alphaville, Every Man for Himslef, and Breathless. My favorite so far is Contempt (which might be predictable given its broader appeal) and Masculine Feminine, and my least favorite is honestly probably Alphaville or Every Man for Himself, though I still found both partially interesting.

From here, I'm wondering: should I go into his 21st century films, seek his experimental stuff in the 70s, go back to the commerical cinema of the 60s? Is "La Chinoise" a bad next step?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just keep watching them, doesn’t matter the order. I didn’t love La Chinoise but also Contempt would be my least favorite of the ones you’ve seen so far, Alphaville probably my favorite. 

But all of his movies are worth watching, he’s amazing. The remaining essentials for you of really really good ones are Vivre Sa Vie and Weekend. His other earlier ones like A Woman is a Woman, Bande A Part, are Le Petit Soldat are decent, still worth watching. King Lear and Hail Mary are awesome in his later career, not sure how much you’d like them though if you didn’t like Every Man for Himself.
  - idk, maybe I just loved Contempt most (aside from the music and cinematography), because despite Godard's clear cynicism for everything on display, I thought the ending felt weirdly hopeful, though that very well may be a misinterpretation on my part. The writer walks away all defeated after a solid 90 minutes of domestic disputes and industry frustration, yet regardless, Frtiz Lang continues to make the weird Odyssey movie, and life goes on. It felt surprisingly optimistic about art and artists, given everything that came beforehand.
2. Start with the rest of his 60s work: Weekend, La Chinoise, A Woman is a Woman, Bande a Part, 2 or 3 Things I Know About Her 

Then you can dip into his more challenging material. 

For his 70s work I'd recommend Tout va Bien and Ici et ailleurs

Then after that there's his post 70s work- the order that you watch it really doesn't matter. Some of my favorites of his from the period include Passion,  Oh Woe is Me, Hail Mary, In Praise of Love. 

Then there's his magnum opus Histoire(s) du Cinema which is pretty unique as far as his work goes.
  - *Histoire(s) du Cinema* is, I think, the best and most important thing he has made. It is largely uninterested in reproducing cinematic conventions in a straightforward way, so it can be somewhat challenging to watch for someone who has only seen his heavily Hollywood-influenced early works, but it says so much about the medium. Anyone who is interested in the possibilities of film should see it.

I would say that *In Praise of Love* and *Germany Year 90 Nine Zero* are also great films from the latter part of his career. I'm sure there are many more I haven't seen. 
3. You should really check out Weekend. It is very unique and really makes you think. For me it is one of my favorite films of all time and my favorite of his films, and I used to say Godard was my favorite filmmaker. (Now I like Yorgos Lanthimos best) 
  - Weekend absolutely. Still one of my favourites of his. 
  - Godards funniest film, although he may have not though that.
  - Watching Weekend in the movie theater was an experience.

My college roommate was a massive Godard fan, which is how I became one. There was a screening of Weekend and he told everyone he knew to watch it, knowing full well that a lot of people wouldn't like it.

He was right. A bunch of people in the audience stood up and left. I stayed and watched the whole thing, but I remember thinking what kind of film is this? All the jump cuts, disjointed sound effects like the honking of cars, the post-apocalyptic feel of a massive car crash, the strange deadpan acting, political digressions.

I wasn't sure I liked it and some parts were very hard to keep watching, but I was intrigued. I later found about about Brechtian distanciation and other modernist techniques that tried to turn passive viewers into more active ones by challenging you and making you uncomfortable, so you start to question things in the film (or play, or piece of art), and its relationship to you and the world you live in. They tried to foreground the medium they were using to highlight the fact this isn't a "real" representation of reality, but a constructed one.

Anyway, stuff like that made me really rethink things about film (and art and literature). Learning how to appreciate Godard's films, which are more like essays about filmmaking, actually made me gain a deeper appreciation for films in general, not just avant-garde experimental stuff.

I love the French New Wave, but Godard probably taught me more about film itself than anyone else.
4. Don’t worry about it. Your personal opinion owes nothing to nobody, and if you can’t stand it, don’t waste your precious time on it. Nobody has a hold on your taste and interest.

I’ve seen maybe 12-15 of Godard movies, and decided, that as “important” as he is in the context of semiotics, and New Wave, and political cinema, that he’s not for me. And the same goes for a bunch of other “seminal” filmmakers. I’ve seen 8 David Lynch movies, and they say nearly nothing to me. I can re-watch True romance, Jackie Brown and Pulp Fiction again and again, and I can’t stand the rest of Tarantino. I’ve seen 7 or 8 of Nolan’s film, and didn’t like them at all. Etc, Etc.

There are hundreds of other better and less famous artists that you can enjoy.
5. What other films/filmmakers from the French new wave movement have you seen? If the answer is none, you’re definitely allowed to say I gave Godard a fair shot and he’s not for me and start watching like Jacques Demy’s major works if those might be more up your alley.

## 5. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (March 15, 2026)

**Score:** 7
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **Requiem for a Dream (2000, Darren Aronofsky) — re-watch** — Still don’t see what’s so great about it. The first time I watched *Requiem for a Dream*, I thought that it had flashes of potential, but was mostly just embarrassingly amateurish, with Aronfosky seemingly being more concerned with finding a spot to awkwardly force in every avant-garde technique he could think of than with telling a well-structured story or making a cohesive point. And now that I’ve seen it again, my opinion remains basically unchanged. Do not recommend. **3/10**

**Little Shop of Horrors (1986, Frank Oz)** — (The director’s cut.) Mixed feelings about this one.

**START OF SPOILERS**

I think that this movie is nearly perfect, and might be even better than the already-pretty-great play that it’s based on… right up to the point where Orin dies. But Oz seems to have decided that the Seymour of the play was too unsympathetic for a movie protagonist, and softens enough of his stupidest and/or most evil actions to the point that very little of what happens seems to actually be his fault. I don’t mind adaptations reworking the plot of the source material, but Seymour’s descent into villainy is so core to the story of *Little Shop of Horrors* that trying to remove it means he barely gets to do anything for the entire second half of the movie. From that point on, the thing feels less like a story with any real point or conflict, and more like it’s just an extended showcase for the Audrey II as a practical effect. To be fair, though, it is a *spectacular* practical effect. It really does manage to carry the movie impressively well, despite the thinness of the story.

**END OF SPOILERS**

Modestly recommended. **7/10**

**The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962, John Ford) — re-watch** — Not sure I have much more to say about this than I did the last time I reviewed it. It’s one of those movies that’s so self-evidently great that it almost feels pointless to try to talk about it. Still a **10/10**.

**Blue Velvet (1986, David Lynch)** — My second David Lynch film, after *The Elephant Man*. I think he might be becoming my new least-favourite of the canonical “great directors”. It’s strange, because both Lynch’s fans and detractors seem to think of him as someone who makes weird, confusing movies that allow for many different interpretations, whereas I find both of the Lynch movies I’ve seen to be frustrating primarily because of how incessantly he beats you over the head with the single interpretation of his work that he will allow. Maybe if I thought the points he was attempting to make were more interesting, I could forgive that, but it all just feels to me like juvenile everyone’s-a-piece-of-shit-except-me cynicism. Isabella Rossellini is really good here, but that’s about all I have to say in the way of praise. Do not recommend. **3/10**

**Movie of the week:** *The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance*
  - I'm not sure how you could conclude that the sole interpretation of Blue Velvet is one of cynicism. One of its last shots is of the robin, explicitly labeled as a symbol for love, eating an insect, a symbol for societal rot, while a song called The Mystery of Love plays. Love triumphs over evil. For as dark as Blue Velvet is, it's starkly optimistic at the end of the day.
  - you’ve chosen two very nonweird lynch films
2. **Gretchen the Greenhorn (1916, dir. Sidney Franklin, Chester M. Franklin)**

A solid immigrant story. Gretchen the Greenhorn tells the story of Gretchen Van Hoek, a Dutch girl who follows in her father's footsteps and tries to make it big in America. She lives in a New York slum with scores of people of different nationalities and races, all of whom are fairly characterized and humanized, something I wasn't fully expecting from a movie of this subject matter from 1916. Perhaps the script is a little too lofty and Romantic at times, but the frank depiction of underserved people from a bevy of different backgrounds forming a strong community effectively demonstrates the origins of millions of Americans. To remind Americans that they are the children of immigrants is something that, for unfortunately uncouth reasons, has seemingly always remained in vogue.

The film's structure in the first half is very loose. There's not much of a plot, and the film takes the time to develop the setting, its characters, and their relationships in a leisurely manner. This changes when a ring of counterfeiters tries extorting the main characters, at which point the film turns into a kind of okay crime drama. It's exciting when the counterfeiters kidnap Gretchen and when the police chase them in a boat, but it's much less interesting than a simple depiction of people living their lives. Still, Gretchen the Greenhorn is good overall and worth a watch.

**The Half-Breed (1916, dir. Allan Dwan)**

The Half-Breed is a lame character drama that reinforces racist mindsets couched inside an anti-racist premise. Douglas Fairbanks plays Lo Dorman, the abandoned son of a white man and a Native American woman. Raised by a recently deceased white hermit, Dorman has to make it in a society that decries non-white people. The biggest problem with The Half-Breed, at least from a screenwriting perspective, is that Dorman is not an interesting character. Fairbanks' performance is pretty subdued, and Dorman has neither a motivation nor any distinguishing traits other than his Native American heritage, which is really hard to infer on account of Fairbanks' race. By the halfway point, Dorman is directionless as a character, and his involvement in the story is limited to his mediation of a lousy love triangle. His racial prejudice arc is no more compelling, and by the end it's apparent that it was never about racial prejudice. The white people of his town stop discriminating against him once they realize that he's the son of one of the people spearheading the discrimination. They realize that it's emotionally inconvenient to discriminate against this specific person of Native American descent, not that racial discrimination is wrong in and of itself. It's pretty sickening, but can I really be surprised? I mean, this movie is called The Half-Breed for crying out loud.

**Barton Fink (1991, dir. Joel Coen, Ethan Coen)**

Knowing that this was a Coen movie, I expected it to be brilliant, and I was not let down. If anything, it subverted my expectations of what a Coen Brothers movie could be in utterly fascinating ways. The story of Barton Fink's attempts to write a wrestling picture was already engrossing, but the surreal, frightening third act elevated the film to new heights. There's no indication in the first two thirds about where the film will go, but the surreal twists never feel gimmicky and always feel earned. It's all in service of a great story about writing, writers, and the systems that corrupt them. It's a simply masterful film, and I know that when I watch it again I'll probably struggle to rate it against Fargo, The Big Lebowski, and the Coens' other masterpieces.

**The Heart of a Hero (1916, dir. Emile Chautard)**

A very melodramatic and convoluted retelling of the life and death of Nathan Hale, a real American intelligence officer executed by the Brits in the Revolutionary War. Unlike the real Nathan Hale, Robert Warwick's version is an older schoolteacher who's in love with one of his students, which breaks the whole film. It's super disgusting that a teacher is having an affair with his student, and doesn't ingratiate the audience towards a man the film wants you to call a hero. It would be one thing if this was isolated in the first act, but Hale's relationship with Alice Adams is pivotally important to the whole story. When the villainous General Howe suspects that Hale is a spy, he uses Adams in a difficult-to-understand ploy to get Hale to break his cover. The second act is all about Howe's attempts to get Adams and Hale in the same room, and all the while you're wondering why Howe just doesn't interrogate Hale directly, something closer to real life.

Although the story is kind of stupid, there is something strangely engrossing about the film, and I think it has to do with the setting. Most 1910s movies I've seen are set in the present day, the Old West, or the Civil War, both of which being relatively recent periods in American history. Some films, like Lois Weber's Hypocrites and The Dumb Girl of Portici, are set in medieval Europe and seventeenth-century Naples respectively, but from what I can tell they were both shot in the US. The Heart of a Hero though is set over a hundred years before most of the audience members were born and was likely shot in New Jersey or New York's Hudson Valley, very close to where the real Revolution was fought. It's not a particularly well-shot or well-acted movie, but I think it's so much more watchable than it has any right being, and I think this tangible rooting in American history has something to do with it. If the story were any good, I'd probably like this movie a lot.

**Resident Evil (2002, dir. Paul WS Anderson)**

I wasn't expecting anything dramatically compelling from this movie, but I was expecting something a lot more entertaining. When Resident Evil is allowed to go crazy, it's fun, but so much of the film feels like a tired, ugly retread of a thousand more interesting films. This feels like filler for the Sci-Fi Channel rather than a theatrical release. And to have a big IP associated with it! How the hell did this piece of shit get five sequels?

**We Bought a Zoo (2011, dir. Cameron Crowe)**

We Bought a Zoo feels like someone's first movie. There are a few really strong ideas and good scenes, but nothing comes together and the film is held together with aggressively earnest sentimentality. As much as I do like sentimentality, if there's no story, then I might as well be watching a YouTube compilation of emotional scenes. The script has a really solid foundation for a story about grief and ambition, but it feels like Crowe wanted to cut to the chase and wring out as much emotion from his audience as quickly as possible. It's an emotionally draining movie; sometimes it's effective, and other times you're watching the clock and looking up Almost Famous quotes on IMDB.
3. **Hamnet (2025, Chloé Zhao)**

Was able to get this in before the Oscars! This was an emotional watch for me, impossible to hold the tears back during the last act. Powerful performances by Jessie Buckley (Agnes) and Jacobi Jupe (Hamnet). Artful and devastating, production was beautiful. **8/10**

**Friday the 13th Part 2 (1981, Steve Miner)**

Had to watch this on Friday the 13th! It was a fun but predictable watch. Campy and cheesy. Great for a sequel, good for a slasher. **6/10**

**Eddington (2025, Ari Aster)**

Big fan of Ari Aster, not a fan of this movie. The story was okay but the pacing was awful. Felt like they were trying too much and it just didn’t land. **4/10**
4. **Death Will Come (2024)** 

This was a random cinema pick this week and it's watchable enough, though how they managed to make an assassin chasing down members of the Luxumbourgian? mob this dreary, I don't know. Marketing made it look more interesting than it was. There's a central mystery that's easily solved before the halfway mark if you're paying attention. 

But I did think the main character, a lesbian ex-working girl turned assassin and her relationship to the old, blind madame was interesting. There's a fun club scene with the two of them, where the blind madame makes her pick up a girl and tell her all about it.

**Turbulence (2025)** 

It's a quasi chamber-piece thriller set on a hot air baloon somewhere in the dolomites. Sadly, it's also not very thrilling and the CGI is not great. Still better than Flight Risk (2025) though, which is my new low-bar for in-the-air thrillers. 

I did like some of the performances (Olga Kurylenko was fun, as was the female main character). 

**Dance To Death (2017)**

This is a russian post-apocalyptic dystopian film that feels very Divergent coded, except there's only two factions, and the elite is making people dance to the death in dubstep dance battles meant to "appease" nature after they fucked up the world with atomic bombs.

It's pure insanity, but I found it quite watchable. Terrible CGI, even for the time though.

Also I really don't like dubstep. Any other music, and I'd have given this a 3.5/5 for sure lol

**Ghost Stories (2017)**

This is a horror anthology type deal that has a very fun frame story about a guy who debunks supernatural stories. First sub-story didn't really work for me, though the latter two, starring Martin Freeman and Alex Lawther are great fun. Immediately recognized Lawther from that Black Mirror Shut Up And Dance episode and he's bringing a similar desperation to this. 

**Marrakesh Cult (1979)**

Amazon Prime is bang on with this eurotrash sexploitation type softcore films these days in my recommendations. This purports to be a crime story about a woman getting sucked into a cult, but then it also makes the baffling decision to have one of the cops looking for her sleep his way through Marrakesh so he can find one of her cult members and ask her to invite the lost girl for a threesome? 

Also very strange music choices. Happy synths where there really shouldn't be any. 

The stuff they shot on location in Marrakesh looks better than the rest of the movie. 
5. [**WEEK # 271:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

MR. SCORSESE (2025) is a terrific portrait of one of the greatest living directors of our time. However, at nearly 5 hours, it's simply too short! 

The director, Rebecca Miller (Arthur's daughter), must have gotten good access to him [maybe through her husband Daniel Day-Lewis, who played in 2 of his movies?]. So she was able to create a sprawling, intimate and moving documentary of his personal life as well as (some of) his 67 movies. From his rough childhood in Little Italy to his current status and acclaim. He appears to be a thoughtful, honest, vulnerable and sensitive person. And his mastery of film language, deep knowledge of film history, his photographic memory, all shine through. It's a must-see for any cineast. 

Items: I forgot that he "co-directed" Woodstock ... When sweet Thelma Schoonmaker makes her first appearance in footage from the mid-60's to help him save his first student films in the editing room ... The interviews with his friend De Nero too are honest and emotional ... 

I must set some time to see the few movies of his that I still haven't seen ('Silence', 'Kundun', 'The color of money', Bringing out the dead'), and return to many of the others ('New York, New York', 'Gangs of New York', 'The last Waltz'(!!) that I have. He also played  (or cameoed) in 264 movies, none of which are mentioned here, and the many productions and projects he's involved with are only given only one brief mention. 
**10/10.** *[Female Director]*

*(I have one non-specific personal pet peeve: I wish that documentaries will not ever use clips from television shows, where news anchors and talking heads are using their "important" voices to mouth platitudes and ask inane questions. I hate to listen to them!...)*

🍿 

"You should know that this man, Martin Scorsese, who everybody thinks is a respected American filmmakers, sleeps with a crucifix above his bed!"

On the other hand, the British **MOVIES ARE MY LIFE** (1978) was the first full-length documentary about Scorsese, and it was terrible on all levels. Everything that was polished and coherent in Miller's film, was like an amateurish home movie in this. But hey, it had cocked-up Robby Robertson, Sounds by Van Morrison, clips from 'The last Waltz', and a very young Jodie Foster remembering how he had to take his "Ulcer pills" all the time. 2/10.

Stephen Frears' **PHILOMENA** (2013) is a moving British drama, based on another true story about the forced adaptation scandal and crimes of the Magdalena Laundries. Old Irish lady Judi Dench is trying to find the son she gave birth to 50 years earlier, who was then sold to adaptive parents by the nuns of the convent. Steve Coogan, acting not quite as his usual insufferable prick, is a fired journalist, who helps her. It's a real tear-jerker. 7/10.

It starts slow and quiet, and is emotionally anchored by the very humane relationship between the little girl and her father. The chomping and grunting and running were not interesting.

On Saturday, the night before the Oscar ceremony, I introduced Ryan Coogler's much-hyped **SINNERS** to my friend, and was surprised at how badly it maintained its allure for me; Even though I saw it only two months ago, I could barely recall much of it.

I much prefer his first two shorts (Locks and Fig. I still haven't seen his 'Fruitvalle Station') and I absolutely hold that it doesn't compare to 'Nickel boys'. **THE GIRL FROM CHICAGO** (1932) is my first by Oscar Micheaux, an early pioneer of "Race films", a genre of films made only for segregated black audiences, made by all-black crews.

It's Pre-Code, a very low-budget story featuring non-professional actors, about a Secret Service agent who falls in love with a Mississippi teacher, and brings her with him to Harlem. It include some musical numbers, including a perplexing one where two male vaudeville dancers are "glued" to one another as they dance (?) It has some excellent scenes, but mostly it is an example of an amateurish, nearly primitive mise-en-scène, interesting for its historic relevance, but not for its artistic quality. 4/10.

🍿 

The Premise of the new **A PRIVATE LIFE** was strong but it turned into a disappointing cliche. It's a Parisian psychological thriller about emotionally-cold psychiatrist Jodi Foster who suspects that her client Virginie Efira was murdered. It also stars her ex-husband Daniel Auteuil and 96 yo Frederick Wiseman (in his last cameo role, just before his death). 

To watch Jodi Foster act in French was lovely, but the unfocused plot and knee-deep motivations behind it created a bland and forgettable mess. 2/10.

This was my 3rd French drama by director Rebecca Zlotowski (after 'Grand Central' and 'Other People's children'). All three started with a great promise and didn't deliver. So I'm not going to look for more of her work. *[Female Director]*.

Still I watched the new adrenaline-thrill ride **MARTY SUPREME**, my first by a Safdie [I tried 'Uncut Gems' once, but it was so nerve-racking that I only lasted 10 minutes].

This too was a hyper-ventilated, super-fast, nervous story of somebody so driven, he's willing to sacrifice everything and everybody to achieve a goal. I can see the appeal for today's young males: An arrogant, charismatic asshole, driven to extremes by toxic ambition. But of course, not somebody I can connect with. Too stressfully-intense. 
So far I've seen 8 of the 10 Best Picture nominees for this year's Oscars, and the only one that really stood out for me was 'One battle after another'. 7/10.

*"FOLLOW THE MONEY" X 2:*

🍿 *“All these neat little houses, and all these nice little streets. It’s hard to believe that something’s wrong with these little houses…”*

A new reassessment that appeared in Salon this week claimed that 50 years later, **ALL THE PRESIDENT’S MEN** feels ["less like a triumphant ode to journalism than a warning about media decay".](https://www.salon.com/2026/03/07/all-the-presidents-men-was-a-warning/) So I had to go back and revisit it, one of my favorite Pakula's 1970's masterpieces. 

And it's truly a brilliant thriller. All of them were icons: Jason Robards with his *"Fuck it, let’s stand by the boys."* Redford and his $100 coiffure. Jack Warden and Martin Balsam's best roles ever. The sounds of all them rotary phones and typewriters clicking. The winning of the good investigating journalists over the evil, corrupt president. Gordon Willis sublime cinematography. The [comparison between television news and newspaper journalism.](https://letterboxd.com/superpulse/film/all-the-presidents-men/) And mostly, the score by David Shire, similar to Pakula's 'The Parallex View' (but which he didn't score!). Another frequent 10/10 re-watch ♻️. 

🍿 I must be a political junkie, because I had to supplement that excellent classic with garbage. Yes, **ALL THE PRESIDENT'S MEN REVISITED** was a rehash of the lazy kind. A bad documentary all around you should watch only to experience schadenfreude one more time. The eye-opening charade of American justice and the rule of law. The only, tiny redeeming irony of it is how everybody was speculating in 2013 if such corruption could ever happen again... Also, it was gratifying to see Nixon's ex-speechwriter Ben Stein literally crying because "Nixon was a Saint who was unjustly maligned".

*"When in Rome - do as the Romanians...".*

**WHEN IN ROME** (2024 ) is a mediocre Danish Tourist-porn drama, about a Danish couple celebrating their 35th wedding anniversary in Rome, where they run into the woman’s old flame. I only picked it because the vivacious old art teacher was played by Rolf Lassgård (of ’After the wedding’). But the two actors I never heard of who played the elderly couple, were so uncharismatically banal, that it was a chore to sit through. ⬇️ Could Not Finish ⬇️.

🍿 

Why do I keep watching old stand-up shows of Louis C.K.?

**OH MY GOD** was his 2013 performance, 4 years before his secret sexual abuses caught up with him publicly. It was a typical Mid display of his usual interests and phobias. During the peak of his career, he was often insightful and extremely funny. But it was obvious even then that he was always punching down, making fun of everything and everybody around him, and basically leaning into being a mean asshole. Same here: A few hilarious jokes (“Of course…, but maybe…”) amid a stream of edgy provocations, cloaked as harmless fun.

But, his **LOUIS C.K. LEARNS ABOUT THE CATHOLIC CHURCH** (2007) was a spot-on look at the church, and their millennium-long tradition of child molestation. He was not mincing words there. Re-watch♻️. 


🍿 
**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 **YOU HIDE ME** (1970. 16 min.) is my first anti-colonial film from Ghana. A walk through the basement of the African Wing at The British Museum, where thousands of stolen artifacts are stored. It's an early exploration of the practice that imperialist armies used to do, looting art and cultural items from the colonies, as part of the subjugation and erasure of the occupied "natives". Essential political viewing. 

🍿 **KID AUTO RACES AT VENICE** (1914. 7 min.) was the first time that Charlie Chaplin played 'The Tramp' in front of the camera. Nicely colorized. Re-watch♻️.  

🍿 **A IS FOR ANT** (2025. 10 min.) is a whimsical British journey through the alphabet, childishly creative. Each letter is done in a different style, some with early-stage surrealistic stop-motion. Wait for the end credits.

🍿 **POP POP CITY** (2024. 10 min.) is a gorgeous Japanese animation about a little girl who receives a robot friend, and whose parents later divorce, and the robot stays with the dad. It later takes a dark, unexpected turn. Recommended. 7/10. 

🍿 **BIRD NAMES** (2026, 22. min.) Is a lovely documentary about some Audubon Society birdwatchers who discuss the current process of renaming about 150 birds. Like Civil War monuments, many of these birds were named after slave-owners and other racist ornithologists from previous centuries, so their names should be changed. All the while they are looking for a sighting of a tiny yellow warbler in the Canadian woods. *[Female Director]*

🍿 **THE STRUGGLE** (1977. 3 min.), my 2nd by Hungarian Marcell Jankovics (after 'Sisyphus'). A sculptor pours his heart and soul into a marble statue, which as it takes shape, starts chiselling him back. 

🍿 **WATCH YOUR WIFE** (1923. 8 min) is a one reel slapstick comedy, which is erroneously listed on YouTube as 'Always tell your wife', Hitchcock's directorial debut. 

🍿 **BOB'S FUNERAL** (2024. 19 min.) is a personal family story about trauma, dick size and family gossip. Everybody seems to be dying, and the survivors struggle to make sense of it. 2/10. 

🍿 

[**(ALL MY FILM REVIEWS - HERE)**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)
6. The Third Man (1949)

This has been on my list for a very very long time. Embarrasingly so. Well worth watching, but I've seen it on top of many best of British lists and think that might be a stretch. Cinematogrpahy is amazing, yes the famed 'dutch angle' does add significntly to the tension. The look of the film is incredible. Acting is fantastic. Story is probably the only 'average' part of the movie, on the surface its pretty basic. Needs to be seen by everyone, it certainly holds the tension and interest. Well worthy of a sunday afternoon, but best British film ever? Hmmmm
